question.text: In the PPM of Damodar Structured Credit AIF (Category II), the section on redemption conditions for close-ended AIFs states:

answer.text: Investors can redeem their units at any time like an open-ended mutual fund
Redemption is available quarterly at NAV minus a 10% exit load
Close-ended AIFs generally do not allow redemption before the end of the fund's tenure, and any early exit is subject to transfer restrictions and manager/trustee consent
SEBI mandates a mandatory redemption window every 6 months for all AIFs

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Close-ended AIFs lock in investor capital for the fund's tenure. Early exit is generally not permitted through redemption — investors must find a transferee, obtain consent, and comply with transfer restrictions. Unlike open-ended mutual funds, there are no periodic redemption windows, and SEBI does not mandate quarterly redemption. **The Trap:** The quarterly redemption option borrows mutual fund concepts (exit loads, quarterly redemption) that do not apply to close-ended AIFs, which operate on a fundamentally different liquidity model. **Cross-Exam Context:** NISM Series V-A contrasts open-ended and close-ended fund liquidity, directly relevant for understanding AIF illiquidity. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** This is the most critical point for investor suitability. Only recommend close-ended AIFs to clients with genuine long-term capital who will not need liquidity for 5-10 years. Illiquidity is the number one source of investor dissatisfaction.
